narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection notice for our program. The admissions model we use evaluates your likelihood of passing the bar exam on your first try, based on key aspects of your academic history and profile. For you specifically, it predicted a 95.8% chance of failing the bar exam, which is why your application was denied—our goal is to admit students with the strongest projected odds of success to maintain our high bar passage rates. The most influential factors pushing toward this failure prediction were your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom tier compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing. Your Year 1 decile, also in the lower range well below average, was the next biggest driver, signaling early academic struggles that the model sees as persistent risks. Your LSAT score of 28, significantly lower than the average of around 37, further reinforced this concern by suggesting challenges with the analytical skills needed for bar success. Even your middle family income bracket played a modest role in tipping the scales toward failure, as higher brackets often correlate with additional resources in the model's view. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 was close to average and nudged slightly toward passing, while your full-time enrollment plan aligned with the vast majority of successful students and offered a small positive boost. Compared to typical applicants who pass, your lower deciles and LSAT stand out as clear departures from their stronger academic showings. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character—it's just the model's data-driven assessment of bar exam patterns. To move forward, we recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades to climb those school deciles, and perhaps exploring part-time options or prep resources tailored to middle-income students like yourself. You're capable of turning this around—many do with focused effort. Keep pursuing your goals; law schools value resilience.
